2. Weakly Supervised Open-Vocabulary Object Detection (AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2023, cites: 18)
   Despite weakly supervised object detection (WSOD) being a promising step toward evading strong instance-level annotations, its capability is confined to closed-set categories within a single training dataset. In this paper, we propose a novel weakly supervised open-vocabulary object detection framework, namely WSOVOD, to extend traditional WSOD to detect novel concepts and utilize diverse datasets with only image-level annotations. To achieve this, we explore three vital strategies, including dataset-level feature adaptation, image-level salient object localization, and region-level vision-language alignment. First, we perform data-aware feature extraction to produce an input-conditional coefficient, which is leveraged into dataset attribute prototypes to identify dataset bias and help achieve cross-dataset generalization. Second, a customized location-oriented weakly supervised region proposal network is proposed to utilize high-level semantic layouts from the category-agnostic segment anything model to distinguish object boundaries. Lastly, we introduce a proposal-concept synchronized multiple-instance network, i.e., object mining and refinement with visual-semantic alignment, to discover objects matched to the text embeddings of concepts. Extensive experiments on Pascal VOC and MS COCO demonstrate that the proposed WSOVOD achieves new state-of-the-art compared with previous WSOD methods in both close-set object localization and detection tasks. Meanwhile, WSOVOD enables cross-dataset and open-vocabulary learning to achieve on-par or even better performance than well-established fully-supervised open-vocabulary object detection (FSOVOD).

3. Open-Vocabulary Object Detection Using Captions (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2020, cites: 579)
   Despite the remarkable accuracy of deep neural networks in object detection, they are costly to train and scale due to supervision requirements. Particularly, learning more object categories typically requires proportionally more bounding box annotations. Weakly supervised and zero-shot learning techniques have been explored to scale object detectors to more categories with less supervision, but they have not been as successful and widely adopted as supervised models. In this paper, we put forth a novel formulation of the object detection problem, namely open-vocabulary object detection, which is more general, more practical, and more effective than weakly supervised and zero-shot approaches. We propose a new method to train object detectors using bounding box annotations for a limited set of object categories, as well as image-caption pairs that cover a larger variety of objects at a significantly lower cost. We show that the proposed method can detect and localize objects for which no bounding box annotation is provided during training, at a significantly higher accuracy than zero-shot approaches. Meanwhile, objects with bounding box annotation can be detected almost as accurately as supervised methods, which is significantly better than weakly supervised baselines. Accordingly, we establish a new state of the art for scalable object detection.

4. Negative Deterministic Information-Based Multiple Instance Learning for Weakly Supervised Object Detection and Segmentation (IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems 2024, cites: 15)
   Weakly supervised object detection (WSOD) and semantic segmentation with image-level annotations have attracted extensive attention due to their high label efficiency. Multiple instance learning (MIL) offers a feasible solution for the two tasks by treating each image as a bag with a series of instances (object regions or pixels) and identifying foreground instances that contribute to bag classification. However, conventional MIL paradigms often suffer from issues, e.g., discriminative instance domination and missing instances. In this article, we observe that negative instances usually contain valuable deterministic information, which is the key to solving the two issues. Motivated by this, we propose a novel MIL paradigm based on negative deterministic information (NDI), termed NDI-MIL, which is based on two core designs with a progressive relation: NDI collection and negative contrastive learning (NCL). In NDI collection, we identify and distill NDI from negative instances online by a dynamic feature bank. The collected NDI is then utilized in a NCL mechanism to locate and punish those discriminative regions, by which the discriminative instance domination and missing instances issues are effectively addressed, leading to improved object- and pixel-level localization accuracy and completeness. In addition, we design an NDI-guided instance selection (NGIS) strategy to further enhance the systematic performance. Experimental results on several public benchmarks, including PASCAL VOC 2007, PASCAL VOC 2012, and MS COCO, show that our method achieves satisfactory performance. The code is available at: https://github.com/GC-WSL/NDI.

   In this work, we propose an open-vocabulary object detection method that, based on image-caption pairs, learns to detect novel object classes along with a given set of known classes. It is a two-stage training approach that first uses a location-guided image-caption matching technique to learn class labels for both novel and known classes in a weakly-supervised manner and second specializes the model for the object detection task using known class annotations. We show that a simple language model fits better than a large contextualized language model for detecting novel objects. Moreover, we introduce a consistency-regularization technique to better exploit image-caption pair information. Our method compares favorably to existing open-vocabulary detection approaches while being data-efficient. Source code is available at https://github.com/lmb-freiburg/locov .

   In this paper, we consider the problem of simultaneously detecting objects and inferring their visual attributes in an image, even for those with no manual annotations provided at the training stage, resembling an open-vocabulary scenario. To achieve this goal, we make the following contributions: (i) we start with a naive two-stage approach for open-vocabulary object detection and attribute classification, termed CLIP-Attr. The candidate objects are first proposed with an offline RPN and later classified for semantic category and attributes; (ii) we combine all available datasets and train with a federated strategy to finetune the CLIP model, aligning the visual representation with attributes, additionally, we investigate the efficacy of leveraging freely available online image-caption pairs under weakly supervised learning; (iii) in pursuit of efficiency, we train a Faster-RCNN type model end-to-end with knowledge distillation, that performs class-agnostic object proposals and classification on semantic categories and attributes with classifiers generated from a text encoder; Finally, (iv) we conduct extensive experiments on VAW, MS-COCO, LSA, and OVAD datasets, and show that recognition of semantic category and attributes is complementary for visual scene understanding, i.e., jointly training object detection and attributes prediction largely outperform existing approaches that treat the two tasks independently, demonstrating strong generalization ability to novel attributes and categories.

7. Cross-Image Co-Attentive Instance Mining for Weakly Supervised Object Detection (2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Industrial Electronics for Sustainable Energy Systems (IESES) 2025, cites: 0)
   Weakly supervised object detection (WSOD), utilizing image-level labels, has attracted considerable attention for its ability to reduce annotation costs. Many approaches tackle the WSOD task as a multiple instance learning (MIL) problem. However, MIL algorithms are likely to concentrate on the most distinctive parts of an object rather than the whole object, which makes the optimization of the MIL algorithms prone to fall into local optima. This phenomenon can be caused by the lack of intra-class diversity within a single image. Concretely, utilizing only single intra-image information may cause networks to concentrate on the most common parts of objects within the same category, but overlooking their diverse parts. To address this challenge, we introduce the CrOss-image CoAttention (COCA) framework, designed to thoroughly investigate cross-image relationships for a more comprehensive extraction of object instances. Specifically, COCA includes a novel co-attention module that captures similarities and differences between objects across imagesa MIL-refinement module for generating pseudo labels, and a regression module for further improving location classification and regression based on pseudo labels. Without bells and whistles, COCA achieves a mean average precision (mAP) 6.4 points higher and a correct localization (CorLoc) 5.8 points higher than online instance classifier refinement (OICR) on PASCAL VOC 2007 datasets.

8. Multiple Instance Complementary Detection and Difficulty Evaluation for Weakly Supervised Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images (IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters 2023, cites: 18)
   Weakly supervised object detection (WSOD) in remote sensing images (RSIs) has attracted a lot of attention because it solely employs image-level labels to drive the model training. Most of the WSOD methods incline to mine salient object as positive instance, and the less salient objects are considered as negative instances, which will cause the problem of missing instances. In addition, the quantity of hard and easy instances is usually imbalanced, and consequently, the cumulative loss of a large amount of easy instances dominates the training loss, which limits the upper bound of WSOD performance. To handle the first problem, a complementary detection network (CDN) is proposed, which consists of a complementary multiple instance detection network (CMIDN) and a complementary feature learning (CFL) module. The CDN can capture robust complementary information from two basic multiple instance detection networks (MIDNs) and mine more object instances. To handle the second problem, an instance difficulty evaluation metric named instance difficulty score (IDS) is proposed, which is employed as the weight of each instance in the training loss. Consequently, the hard instances will be assigned larger weights according to the IDS, which can improve the upper bound of WSOD performance. The ablation experiments demonstrate that our method significantly increases the baseline method by large margins, i.e., 23.6% (10.2%) mean average precision (mAP) and 32.4% (13.1%) correct localization (CorLoc) gains on the NWPU VHR-10.v2 (DIOR) dataset. Our method obtains 58.1% (26.7%) mAP and 72.4% (47.9%) CorLoc on the NWPU VHR-10.v2 (DIOR) dataset, which achieves better performance compared with seven advanced WSOD methods.

9. C-MIL: Continuation Multiple Instance Learning for Weakly Supervised Object Detection (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2019, cites: 256)
   Weakly supervised object detection (WSOD) is a challenging task when provided with image category supervision but required to simultaneously learn object locations and object detectors. Many WSOD approaches adopt multiple instance learning (MIL) and have non-convex loss functions which are prone to get stuck into local minima (falsely localize object parts) while missing full object extent during training. In this paper, we introduce a continuation optimization method into MIL and thereby creating continuation multiple instance learning (C-MIL), with the intention of alleviating the non-convexity problem in a systematic way. We partition instances into spatially related and class related subsets, and approximate the original loss function with a series of smoothed loss functions defined within the subsets. Optimizing smoothed loss functions prevents the training procedure falling prematurely into local minima and facilitates the discovery of Stable Semantic Extremal Regions (SSERs) which indicate full object extent. On the PASCAL VOC 2007 and 2012 datasets, C-MIL improves the state-of-the-art of weakly supervised object detection and weakly supervised object localization with large margins.

10. Mask-Free OVIS: Open-Vocabulary Instance Segmentation without Manual Mask Annotations (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2023, cites: 22)
   Existing instance segmentation models learn task-specific information using manual mask annotations from base (training) categories. These mask annotations require tremendous human effort, limiting the scalability to annotate novel (new) categories. To alleviate this problem, Open-Vocabulary (OV) methods leverage large-scale image-caption pairs and vision-language models to learn novel categories. In summary, an OV method learns task-specific information using strong supervision from base annotations and novel category information using weak supervision from image-captions pairs. This difference between strong and weak supervision leads to overfitting on base categories, resulting in poor generalization towards novel categories. In this work, we overcome this issue by learning both base and novel categories from pseudo-mask annotations generated by the vision-language model in a weakly supervised manner using our proposed Mask-free OVIS pipeline. Our method automatically generates pseudo-mask annotations by leveraging the localization ability of a pre-trained vision-language model for objects present in image-caption pairs. The generated pseudo-mask annotations are then used to supervise an instance segmentation model, freeing the entire pipeline from any labour-expensive instance-level annotations and overfitting. Our extensive experiments show that our method trained with just pseudo-masks significantly improves the mAP scores on the MS-COCO dataset and OpenImages dataset compared to the recent state-of-the-art methods trained with manual masks. Codes and models are provided in https://vibashan.github.io/ovis-web/.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Object detectors fail on rare classes with few or no labeled
instances; how can we detect and localize long-tail or novel
objects without per-class supervision?


### Proposed method
We propose ReLENT: Region-level Entailment and Novel Term Expansion for open-vocabulary object discovery. ReLENT decouples objectness from naming and replaces cosine-similarity classification with a triadic, region-level vision–language NLI head trained under weak and structured supervision. A class-agnostic set-prediction detector first proposes K regions using a ViT backbone with a DETR-style decoder trained only for objectness and box regression. Each region feature r and a candidate phrase p are fed to a cross-attentional NLI head that outputs entail/contradict/unknown probabilities. To regularize semantic granularity without per-class boxes, the NLI head is multi-resolution: alongside the fine phrase decision, it jointly predicts hypernym categories and attribute tokens derived from lexical resources, with consistency constraints between levels.

Supervision is constructed from image–text corpora without per-class boxes by combining multiple weak signals. From captions, we extract noun/attribute phrases via SRL and form image-level entailment labels; we generate counterfactual negatives by semantically controlled perturbations (e.g., role-swaps, attribute flips) that remain fluent but are false for the image. We train the NLI head with multiple-instance learning: for entailed phrases, at least one region must entail; for negatives, all regions should contradict. To sharpen boundaries and reduce spurious matches, we adopt token-by-token negative supervision: for each region, we sample many non-evidential tokens and phrases and explicitly teach the model to assign the ‘contradict’ label, while BIO-style masking around high-heat patches enforces tighter spatial support. Where available, we transfer grounding from QA-linked segmentation data by converting linked segmentations into positive region–phrase entail examples and hard negatives from non-linked but nearby segments; this anchors the MIL objective with sparse, precise supervision. A multi-resolution auxiliary loss enforces that if a fine phrase entails a region, its hypernyms and key attributes also entail, providing structured regularization across label granularities.

To name long-tail and novel objects, ReLENT performs retrieval-augmented vocabulary expansion on-the-fly. Given an image (and optionally high-confidence coarse hypernyms from the NLI head), we build a text query from pooled region descriptors and retrieve a diverse set of candidate phrases and definitions from a large FAISS index of noun/attribute strings and KB synsets. Each candidate’s retrieval score is discretized into confidence bins and embedded as an additional signal in the text encoder, allowing the NLI head to contextually modulate its decisions by source reliability. The retrieved candidates are then scored by the region-level NLI head to produce entailment maps; for phrases that entail without mapping to any known taxonomy, we output them directly or assemble descriptive names by composing top-entailed attributes.

To actively surface novel concepts with minimal passes, we maintain a small ensemble of phrase-proposer policies (particles) that, conditioned on current region features and NLI posteriors, each selects a batch of next phrases to test from the index. The particles are trained with a Bayesian objective to maximize expected information gain—i.e., reduction in uncertainty over region–phrase entailment—regularized to a broad prior so they explore different semantic neighborhoods. We approximate this posterior over policies with SVGD, where policy gradients push particles toward high-gain strategies while a kernel repulsion term preserves diversity. At inference, ReLENT alternates between (1) proposing regions, (2) retrieving and proposing phrases, (3) evaluating with the NLI head, and (4) updating a per-image belief state until a budget is reached. The final detections are boxes whose fine phrases entail and whose hypernym and attribute heads are consistent; when no name cleanly entails, we return attribute-rich descriptions and mark them as novel.

### Rationale
ReLENT avoids per-class box supervision and the well-trodden word–region cosine classification by reframing open-vocabulary detection as region-level entailment with structured weak supervision and retrieval. The MIL-trained NLI head uses only image-level captions plus counterfactual negatives and sparse QA-grounded anchors to learn to localize textual evidence, which naturally transfers to unseen categories. Retrieval-augmented term expansion equips the model with a dynamic, image-conditional vocabulary far beyond any fixed label set, while embedding retrieval confidence stabilizes decisions under noise. The multi-resolution heads regularize semantics via hypernyms and attributes, improving generalization on long-tail classes where fine labels are rare but coarse categories and attributes are common.

The active, SVGD-based phrase proposer maximizes information gain over the NLI outputs and maintains diverse querying strategies, allowing the system to discover and name novel objects efficiently without exhaustive scoring of a massive vocabulary. Token-by-token negative supervision and sparse QA-grounding improve boundary fidelity and reduce over-acceptance—key failure modes when labels are scarce—yielding a detector that can detect and localize rare or novel objects through entailment rather than per-class supervision.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [LLM_NLP] Rethinking Negative Instances for Generative Named Entity Recognition (2402.16602)
   problem: Generative NER methods typically use entity-centric training that ignores non-entity tokens, limiting contextual learning and weakening label boundaries. This leads to unlabeled, noisy, and boundary errors and poor zero-shot generalization.
   method:  GNER introduces a token-by-token instruction-tuning schema that labels every token in the sentence, explicitly incorporating negative instances ('O') as context and adding BIO tagging to strengthen boundaries. The prompt pairs each token with its predicted label, with regularization via class-order shuffling and external entity sampling. This reduces noisy and boundary errors (Table 1) and yields strong gains in zero-shot NER, e.g., LLaMA-7B improves by ~12.7 F1 over UniNER on CrossNER while sharing the same backbone and data (Table 2; lines 177, 101–112, 127–129, 261).

2. [MULTIMODAL] Unicoder-VL: A Universal Encoder for Vision and Language by Cross-modal Pre-training (1908.06066)
   problem: Existing CV and NLP pretraining fail to jointly model fine-grained interactions between image regions and long natural-language sequences, limiting performance on cross-modal tasks like retrieval and visual commonsense reasoning.
   method:  Unicoder-VL is a single-stream multi-layer Transformer (initialized from BERT) that ingests wordpieces together with region-level visual features and 5-D box geometry from a frozen Faster R-CNN (top-100 RoIs), projecting visual and location features into a shared space with layer normalization. It is cross-modally pre-trained on 3.8M image–caption pairs with three tasks: (i) Masked Language Modeling masking 15% of words and predicting them conditioned on all image regions; (ii) Masked Object Classification masking 15% of regions (90% replaced with zero vectors) and predicting detector-derived object labels from cross-modal context; (iii) Visual–Linguistic Matching using the [CLS] representation to classify matched vs. mismatched pairs with both negative-image and negative-caption sampling. This yields context-aware, aligned token and region embeddings that fuse vision and language early.

3. [LLM_NLP] Tailor: Generating and Perturbing Text with Semantic Controls (2107.07150)
   problem: NLI models over-rely on lexical-overlap heuristics; prior data augmentation methods improve robustness but are template-bound and limited by specific syntactic configurations.
   method:  Using Tailor’s semantic-role controls, SWAP_CORE perturbations systematically invert agent/patient roles to generate challenging hypotheses for SNLI with minimal augmentation (≈2% of train). Augmenting with these perturbations improves HANS accuracy by about 5.8 points overall and 29.2 points on non-entailment without reducing in-domain SNLI accuracy, outperforming a prior syntactic augmentation baseline. This shows semantically controlled, fine-grained edits can more broadly target heuristic failures than template-based methods.

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

6. [MULTIMODAL] DetCLIPv2: Scalable Open-Vocabulary Object Detection Pre-training via Word-Region Alignment (2304.04514)
   problem: Open-vocabulary detection needs to learn from massive web image–text pairs, but captions lack instance-level boxes, VL distillation suffers domain gaps, and pseudo-labeling limits accuracy and scalability.
   method:  DetCLIPv2 performs end-to-end joint pre-training on detection, grounding, and raw image–text pairs under a unified formulation, learning fine-grained word–region alignment directly from captions. For each noun phrase, it matches the best region proposal via a max-similarity (1-to-1) set alignment and optimizes a text-to-image contrastive loss only, which mitigates the partial-label problem in web data. It selects proposals for contrast by scoring regions with the maximum similarity to all concepts in the local batch, focusing on text-relevant regions. Alternating data types during training creates a flywheel between localization and recognition, and using low-resolution inputs for web pairs enables efficient scaling (e.g., 13× more pairs with similar training time).

7. [CV] LUWA Dataset: Learning Lithic Use-Wear Analysis on Microscopic Images (2403.13171)
   problem: Real deployments face scarce labeled microscopic images and newly discovered categories, necessitating effective few-shot classification and an assessment of whether large vision-language models can aid expert-like reasoning.
   method:  The paper evaluates a simple pretrain + ProtoNet few-shot pipeline across multiple encoders (ResNet, ConvNeXt, ViT/DeiT, CLIP, DINO) over 600 tasks in 9-way-5/20-shot settings, analyzing magnification and modality impacts; and conducts GPT-4V case studies using archaeologist-crafted prompts for reasoning and few-shot classification. DINO-based encoders excel overall, with 50× texture consistently outperforming other settings in scarce-data regimes. GPT-4V can follow expert reasoning cues but performs poorly on few-shot classification, highlighting a gap between VLM reasoning and domain-specific visual discrimination.

8. [MULTIMODAL] Open-Vocabulary Camouflaged Object Segmentation (2311.11241)
   problem: Open-vocabulary dense prediction benchmarks are not tailored to imperceptible, camouflaged objects, making it hard to evaluate models’ ability to generalize to unseen classes in complex scenes.
   method:  They define a new task, Open-Vocabulary Camouflaged Object Segmentation (OVCOS), and release OVCamo: 11,483 hand-selected images with fine masks over 75 relabeled, semantically consistent classes. The split uses 14 seen classes for training and 61 unseen for testing (7713/3770 images), with dataset attributes (e.g., object concentration, color ratio, size) analyzed to capture camouflage difficulty. They adapt evaluation to jointly assess classification and segmentation (e.g., cSm, cFωβ, cMAE, cIoU).

9. [CV] An End-to-End Transformer Model for 3D Object Detection (2109.08141)
   problem: State-of-the-art 3D object detectors on point clouds rely on hand-crafted 3D operators, tuned radii, and multi-stage heuristics (e.g., Hough voting and NMS), which complicate end-to-end learning and portability.
   method:  3DETR is an end-to-end Transformer that operates directly on point clouds with minimal 3D-specific modifications. A standard Transformer encoder processes downsampled points (no encoder positional embeddings), and a parallel Transformer decoder predicts boxes using non-parametric queries sampled via Farthest Point Sampling whose XYZ are embedded with Fourier positional encodings; boxes are predicted as offsets from query locations along with size, orientation, and class. Training uses a DETR-style set prediction objective with Hungarian matching and a geometric cost (GIoU + center distance) plus semantic terms, which discourages duplicates and removes the need for NMS. Ablations show non-parametric queries and Fourier embeddings are critical (large AP gains over parametric queries), and the model outperforms a strong VoteNet baseline on ScanNetV2 by 9.5 AP50 while being simple to implement.

10. [ROBOTICS] Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation (1909.12906)
   problem: Policies trained in simulation often fail to transfer to real robots under unknown or varying dynamics; domain-randomized policies can adapt unpredictably and even deteriorate during on-policy updates on hardware.
   method:  Use a gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning framework (MAML-like) that trains a meta-policy across tasks defined by randomized dynamics, explicitly optimizing expected return after N inner-loop adaptation steps. At deployment, the policy performs K-rollout inner-loop updates with vanilla policy gradient to quickly adapt to the current dynamics, while the outer meta-update backpropagates through the inner steps and is optimized with PPO. Experiments in simulation and on a KUKA puck-hitting task show stable, consistent adaptation and better performance than a domain-randomization baseline.

11. [ROBOTICS] Visual Affordance Prediction for Guiding Robot Exploration (2305.17783)
   problem: Self-supervised robot exploration for learning goal-conditioned manipulation policies requires diverse, plausible, and reachable goals; curiosity-based exploration or in-domain goal generators often fail to cover such goals and generalize poorly.
   method:  Use the learned visual affordance model as a goal-sampling distribution during data collection: at episode start, condition on the current view to sample a goal image, execute the current policy, and store the trajectory. Train a single goal-conditioned policy via behavior cloning with hindsight goal relabeling by setting each trajectory’s goal to its final image (o_g = o_T) and maximizing log π(a_t | o_t, o_g). Without any fine-tuning of the affordance model in the lab, this pipeline drives richer exploration and yields higher success on manipulation tasks (pushing, pick-and-place, stacking) than curiosity and CVAE/Pix2Pix goal samplers.

12. [RL] Gold Seeker: Information Gain from Policy Distributions for Goal-oriented Vision-and-Langauge Reasoning (1812.06398)
   problem: RL-based seekers for goal-oriented vision-and-language tasks typically rely on a single policy, limiting strategic diversity and failing to capture the multimodal nature of effective query generation. This makes exploration inefficient and prevents the agent from reasoning over uncertainty in its own knowledge.
   method:  Maintain a Bayesian distribution over the seeker’s policy parameters rather than a single policy, sampling multiple query hypotheses per state. Formulate the posterior over policies as π(θ|C+,I) ∝ exp(J(θ)/α) π0(θ), where α controls exploration, and optimize the expected return with KL regularization to the prior. Approximate this posterior with Stein Variational Gradient Descent (SVGD) over a set of particles, using policy gradients ∇θJ(θ) to drive particles toward high-reward regions while a kernel repulsion term preserves diversity across modes. This yields diverse RNN-based query generators (particles) that better cover multiple strategies and reduce collapse to a single mode.

13. [IR_REC] Semi-supervised Collaborative Filtering by Text-enhanced Domain Adaptation (2007.07085)
   problem: Cross-domain recommendation on sparse implicit-feedback data without user/item overlap suffers from severe data sparsity and label noise from negative sampling; directly aligning latent embeddings across domains is sub-optimal because different datasets induce different latent spaces.
   method:  Propose TDAR, a text-enhanced domain adaptation framework that uses domain-invariant textual features from reviews as anchors to align source and target. It concatenates fixed textual features with trainable user/item embeddings and feeds them to domain classifiers; embeddings are adversarially trained to confuse the classifiers so user/item representations align across domains in a shared latent-textual space (with separate user/item adaptation nets). On the target domain, training uses only positive labels plus the domain-adaptation signal, abandoning negative sampling to avoid label noise, while the denser source domain supplies negative supervision. This semi-supervised scheme transfers distributional patterns from a dense source to a sparse target and mitigates mislabeling on the target.

14. [IR_REC] Probabilistic Metric Learning with Adaptive Margin for Top-K Recommendation (2101.04849)
   problem: Existing metric-learning recommenders often neglect explicit user-user and item-item relations, leading to embeddings that fail to cluster similar users or items.
   method:  Compute user-user and item-item similarities from the implicit feedback matrix (e.g., cosine similarity with thresholds to define neighbors), and add two margin ranking losses with adaptive margins to explicitly pull similar users/items closer than random ones. These auxiliary losses share the adaptive-margin generation mechanism and are combined with the main user-item objective, producing substantial gains shown in ablations and overall results.

15. [IR_REC] Where to Go Next for Recommender Systems? ID- vs. Modality-based Recommender Models Revisited (2303.13835)
   problem: Do modality-based recommender models (that encode item content such as text or images) truly rival or surpass traditional ID-based models in regular and warm-start item recommendation, beyond cold-start cases? Prior work lacked fair, apples-to-apples comparisons under identical backbones and settings.
   method:  Perform a rigorous head-to-head study by replacing itemID embeddings with strong pre-trained modality encoders (BERT/RoBERTa for text; ResNet/Swin for images) and training end-to-end within identical recommender backbones (two-tower DSSM vs sequential SASRec) using the same loss, sampling, and hyperparameter protocol, with a DT-layer for dimension matching. Across three large-scale datasets (MIND text; HM and Bili images), results show that with the SASRec (seq2seq) backbone, MoRec matches or outperforms IDRec for text and is on par for vision even in warm-start settings, whereas with DSSM MoRec lags behind IDRec (e.g., Table 2, Table 3; lines 153–161, 169–180).

16. [SPEECH] Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers (2004.14840)
   problem: ASR target choices (characters vs subwords) have complementary error patterns; single-resolution training can overfit and underperform on rare words or syntax.
   method:  Use multiresolution multitask learning: a shared Transformer decoder predicts both character- and subword-level transcriptions, with separate output layers, trained via a weighted sum of losses (γ controls the balance; γ=0.5 performs best). This acts as regularization and reduces decoding steps for subwords while retaining fine-grained character supervision, yielding around 50% faster convergence and up to ~18% relative WER improvement over subword-only models on How2.

17. [SPEECH] RED-ACE: Robust Error Detection for ASR using Confidence Embeddings (2203.07172)
   problem: ASR error detection models typically rely only on the transcription text and ignore auxiliary ASR signals like word-level confidences, limiting their ability to accurately flag recognition errors.
   method:  Introduce RED-ACE, a BERT-based sequence tagger augmented with a confidence embedding layer. ASR word-level confidence scores are quantized into bins and mapped via a learned embedding matrix; the confidence embedding is summed with the token, segment, and position embeddings to form each token’s input. This joint, contextualized encoding of text and confidence yields statistically significant F1 gains over text-only BERT and over naive score fusion (e.g., appending raw scores, BERT C) on LibriSpeech clean/other (e.g., +4.9% and +7.4% F1), remains robust under cross-pool shifts (+6.3% and +9.9% F1), and generalizes across different ASR models (+2.6% and +3.8% F1).

18. [SPEECH] Retrieval Augmented Generation based context discovery for ASR (2509.19567)
   problem: Contextual ASR performance degrades on rare and out-of-vocabulary words, but automatically discovering the right context without modifying/fine-tuning the ASR model or relying on structured catalogs remains challenging.
   method:  Introduce CB-RAG: a model-agnostic, embedding-based retrieval method that builds a query from the concatenated transcripts of the previous k VAD-detected segments and encodes it with a MiniLM sentence transformer (all-MiniLM-L6-v2). It retrieves Top-N nearest words via cosine similarity in a FAISS index over a large, frozen vocabulary of 466k non-stopword entries (augmented with definitions when available), and passes these as context to a black-box contextual biasing ASR. Across TED-LIUMv3, Earnings21, and SPGISpeech, CB-RAG reduces WER by up to ~17% relative to no-context while keeping latency close to baseline (≈1.02–1.36×).

19. [ROBOTICS] Implicit Kinematic Policies: Unifying Joint and Cartesian Action Spaces in End-to-End Robot Learning (2203.01983)
   problem: End-to-end visuomotor policies are highly sensitive to the choice of action space (joints vs. Cartesian), and explicit policies that try to use multiple spaces face conflicting gradients and loss-balancing issues, making it hard for a single model to exploit the strengths of each space across diverse manipulation tasks.
   method:  Implicit Kinematic Policies (IKP) embed forward kinematics as a differentiable module inside an implicit behavioral cloning policy (energy-based model). Actions are sampled in joint space, transformed via FK to consistent Cartesian representations, and both joint and Cartesian actions (including 6-DoF poses for every robot link) are concatenated with observations and fed into an autoregressive EBM; sampling-based, derivative-free optimization selects low-energy actions dimension-by-dimension. This kinematically consistent, multi-action-space input lets the model discover and leverage low-frequency structure present in either representation without loss weighting or IK solvers, yielding performance on par with or better than the best single-space baselines (e.g., 85.9% sweeping, 97.5% flipping).

20. [ROBOTICS] Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation (1909.12906)
   problem: Policies trained in simulation often fail to transfer to real robots under unknown or varying dynamics; domain-randomized policies can adapt unpredictably and even deteriorate during on-policy updates on hardware.
   method:  Use a gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning framework (MAML-like) that trains a meta-policy across tasks defined by randomized dynamics, explicitly optimizing expected return after N inner-loop adaptation steps. At deployment, the policy performs K-rollout inner-loop updates with vanilla policy gradient to quickly adapt to the current dynamics, while the outer meta-update backpropagates through the inner steps and is optimized with PPO. Experiments in simulation and on a KUKA puck-hitting task show stable, consistent adaptation and better performance than a domain-randomization baseline.

21. [ROBOTICS] Robust Instant Policy: Leveraging Student's t-Regression Model for Robust In-context Imitation Learning of Robot Manipulation (2506.15157)
   problem: LLM-based in-context imitation learning for robot manipulation can hallucinate, producing outlier trajectories that deviate from few-shot demonstrations, leading to failures in continuous control.
   method:  Robust Instant Policy (RIP) queries an LLM instant policy (via KAT) multiple times on the same context to sample Q candidate trajectories, then fits a Student's t-regression model Sθ(a_t|t;ν) with neural networks for mean and variance to the sampled actions over time, leveraging heavy-tailed robustness to outliers. The final plan is the time-wise mean of the fitted t-distribution, effectively ignoring hallucinated trajectories. Ablations show ν=1.5 outperforms Gaussian (ν→∞), and RIP improves success rates by 26% over KAT and 16% over a Gaussian aggregator across 10 manipulation tasks, especially in low-data regimes.

1. Hierarchical Multimodal Knowledge Matching for Training-Free Open-Vocabulary Object Detection. (IEEE Transactions on Image Processing 2025, cites: 2)
   Open-Vocabulary Object Detection (OVOD) aims to leverage the generalization capabilities of pre-trained vision-language models for detecting objects beyond the trained categories. Existing methods mostly focus on supervised learning strategies based on available training data, which might be suboptimal for data-limited novel categories. To tackle this challenge, this paper presents a Hierarchical Multimodal Knowledge Matching method (HMKM) to better represent novel categories and match them with region features. Specifically, HMKM includes a set of object prototype knowledge that is obtained using limited category-specific images, acting as off-the-shelf category representations. In addition, HMKM also includes a set of attribute prototype knowledge to represent key attributes of categories at a fine-grained level, with the goal to distinguish one category from its visually similar ones. During inference, two sets of object and attribute prototype knowledge are adaptively combined to match categories with region features. The proposed HMKM is training-free and can be easily integrated as a plug-and-play module into existing OVOD models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our HMKM significantly improves the performance when detecting novel categories across various backbones and datasets.

3. Open-Vocabulary Crack Object Detection Through Attribute-Guided Similarity Probing (Applied Sciences 2025, cites: 0)
   Timely detection of road surface defects such as cracks and potholes is critical for ensuring traffic safety and reducing infrastructure maintenance costs. While recent advances in image-based deep learning techniques have shown promise for automated road defect detection, existing models remain limited to closed-set detection settings, making it difficult to recognize newly emerging or fine-grained defect types. To address this limitation, we propose an attribute-aware open-vocabulary crack detection (AOVCD) framework, which leverages the alignment capability of pretrained vision–language models to generalize beyond fixed class labels. In this framework, crack types are represented as combinations of visual attributes, enabling semantic grounding between image regions and natural language descriptions. To support this, we extend the existing PPDD dataset with attribute-level annotations and incorporate a multi-label attribute recognition task as an auxiliary objective. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed AOVCD model outperforms existing baselines. In particular, compared to CLIP-based zero-shot inference, the proposed model achieves approximately a 10-fold improvement in average precision (AP) for novel crack categories. Attribute classification performance—covering geometric, spatial, and textural features—also increases by 40% in balanced accuracy (BACC) and 23% in AP. These results indicate that integrating structured attribute information enhances generalization to previously unseen defect types, especially those involving subtle visual cues. Our study suggests that incorporating attribute-level alignment within a vision–language framework can lead to more adaptive and semantically grounded defect recognition systems.

4. 3D-AffordanceLLM: Harnessing Large Language Models for Open-Vocabulary Affordance Detection in 3D Worlds (International Conference on Learning Representations 2025, cites: 24)
   3D Affordance detection is a challenging problem with broad applications on various robotic tasks. Existing methods typically formulate the detection paradigm as a label-based semantic segmentation task. This paradigm relies on predefined labels and lacks the ability to comprehend complex natural language, resulting in limited generalization in open-world scene. To address these limitations, we reformulate the traditional affordance detection paradigm into \textit{Instruction Reasoning Affordance Segmentation} (IRAS) task. This task is designed to output a affordance mask region given a query reasoning text, which avoids fixed categories of input labels. We accordingly propose the \textit{3D-AffordanceLLM} (3D-ADLLM), a framework designed for reasoning affordance detection in 3D open-scene. Specifically, 3D-ADLLM introduces large language models (LLMs) to 3D affordance perception with a custom-designed decoder for generating affordance masks, thus achieving open-world reasoning affordance detection. In addition, given the scarcity of 3D affordance datasets for training large models, we seek to extract knowledge from general segmentation data and transfer it to affordance detection. Thus, we propose a multi-stage training strategy that begins with a novel pre-training task, i.e., \textit{Referring Object Part Segmentation}~(ROPS). This stage is designed to equip the model with general recognition and segmentation capabilities at the object-part level. Then followed by fine-tuning with the IRAS task, 3D-ADLLM obtains the reasoning ability for affordance detection. In summary, 3D-ADLLM leverages the rich world knowledge and human-object interaction reasoning ability of LLMs, achieving approximately an 8\% improvement in mIoU on open-vocabulary affordance detection tasks.

5. OVA-DETR: Open Vocabulary Aerial Object Detection Using Image-Text Alignment and Fusion (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 8)
   <no abstract>

6. Dual Context-Conditioned Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation in Remote Sensing (IEEE Access 2026, cites: 0)
   Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) aims to assign pixel-level labels to unseen categories during training, offering a flexible and scalable solution for real-world applications. Although vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP have achieved remarkable success in natural images, the extension of OVSS to remote sensing remains challenging due to heterogeneous landscapes, large-scale variations, and small objects in complex backgrounds. To preserve spatial details for improving the segmentation performance on small objects in remote sensing images, existing methods typically adopt tile-based inference with sliding windows rather than downscaling entire scenes. However, processing each tile independently without considering surrounding contexts often leads to incorrect predictions due to the absence of global scene understanding and the discontinuities at tile borders. Here, we propose training-free dual context-conditioned OVSS specifically tailored for remote sensing. Our proposed dual context conditions consist of inter-region context integration and global context integration and are incorporated into the last attention layer of the CLIP image encoder via our frequency-aware gating mechanism, enabling joint refinement of attention weights and value representations to balance between fine details and scene-level structures. Specifically, the inter-region context integration employs vision foundation models (VFMs) to measure patch-level similarities between a target tile and its neighboring tiles, providing complementary local neighboring cues and the global context integration leverages a downsampled whole image to capture structural cues via VFMs, enabling the model to incorporate global information cues. Since the reliability of local and global cues differs across scenes, our frequency-aware gating mechanism adaptively modulates their relative importance based on the image’s frequency composition. Extensive experiments on 17 benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance for training-free OVSS in remote sensing, consistently outperforming prior arts on all benchmarks.

7. Multiple Large AI Models’ Consensus for Object Detection—A Survey (Applied Sciences 2025, cites: 1)
   The rapid development of large artificial intelligence (AI) models—large language models (LLMs), multimodel large language models (MLLMs) and vision–language models (VLMs)—has enabled instruction-driven visual understanding, where a single foundation model can recognize and localize arbitrary objects from natural-language prompts. However, predictions from individual models remain inconsistent—LLMs hallucinate nonexistent entities, while VLMs exhibit limited recall and unstable calibration compared to purpose-trained detectors. To address these limitations, a new paradigm termed “multiple large AI model’s consensus” has emerged. In this approach, multiple heterogeneous LLMs, MLLMs or VLMs process a shared visual–textual instruction and generate independent structured outputs (bounding boxes and categories). Next, their results are merged through consensus mechanisms. This cooperative inference improves spatial accuracy and semantic correctness, making it particularly suitable for generating high-quality training datasets for fast real-time object detectors. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of the large multi-AI model’s consensus for object detection. We formalize the concept, review related literature on ensemble reasoning and multimodal perception, and categorize existing methods into four frameworks: prompt-level, reasoning-to-detection, box-level, and hybrid consensus. We further analyze fusion algorithms, evaluation metrics, and benchmark datasets, highlighting their strengths and limitations. Finally, we discuss open challenges—vocabulary alignment, uncertainty calibration, computational efficiency, and bias propagation—and identify emerging trends such as consensus-aware training, structured reasoning, and collaborative perception ecosystems.

9. RegionCLIP: Region-based Language-Image Pretraining (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2021, cites: 847)
   Contrastive language-image pretraining (CLIP) using image-text pairs has achieved impressive results on image classification in both zero-shot and transfer learning set-tings. However, we show that directly applying such mod-els to recognize image regions for object detection leads to unsatisfactory performance due to a major domain shift: CLIP was trained to match an image as a whole to a text de-scription, without capturing the fine-grained alignment be-tween image regions and text spans. To mitigate this issue, we propose a new method called RegionCLIP that signifi-cantly extends CLIP to learn region-level visual representations, thus enabling fine-grained alignment between image regions and textual concepts. Our method leverages a CLIP model to match image regions with template captions, and then pretrains our model to align these region-text pairs in the feature space. When transferring our pretrained model to the open-vocabulary object detection task, our method outperforms the state of the art by 3.8 AP50 and 2.2 AP for novel categories on COCO and LVIS datasets, respectively. Further, the learned region representations support zero-shot inference for object detection, showing promising results on both COCO and LVIS datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/microsoft/RegionCLIP.

   Pixel-level recognition of objects manipulated by the user from egocentric images enables key applications spanning assistive technologies, industrial safety, and activity monitoring. However, progress in this area is currently hindered by the scarcity of annotated datasets, as existing approaches rely on costly manual labels. In this paper, we propose to learn human-object interaction detection leveraging narrations $\unicode{x2013}$ natural language descriptions of the actions performed by the camera wearer which contain clues about manipulated objects. We introduce Narration-Supervised in-Hand Object Segmentation (NS-iHOS), a novel task where models have to learn to segment in-hand objects by learning from natural-language narrations in a weakly-supervised regime. Narrations are then not employed at inference time. We showcase the potential of the task by proposing Weakly-Supervised In-hand Object Segmentation from Human Narrations (WISH), an end-to-end model distilling knowledge from narrations to learn plausible hand-object associations and enable in-hand object segmentation without using narrations at test time. We benchmark WISH against different baselines based on open-vocabulary object detectors and vision-language models. Experiments on EPIC-Kitchens and Ego4D show that WISH surpasses all baselines, recovering more than 50% of the performance of fully supervised methods, without employing fine-grained pixel-wise annotations. Code and data can be found at https://fpv-iplab.github.io/WISH.

   Despite weakly supervised object detection (WSOD) being a promising step toward evading strong instance-level annotations, its capability is confined to closed-set categories within a single training dataset. In this paper, we propose a novel weakly supervised open-vocabulary object detection framework, namely WSOVOD, to extend traditional WSOD to detect novel concepts and utilize diverse datasets with only image-level annotations. To achieve this, we explore three vital strategies, including dataset-level feature adaptation, image-level salient object localization, and region-level vision-language alignment. First, we perform data-aware feature extraction to produce an input-conditional coefficient, which is leveraged into dataset attribute prototypes to identify dataset bias and help achieve cross-dataset generalization. Second, a customized location-oriented weakly supervised region proposal network is proposed to utilize high-level semantic layouts from the category-agnostic segment anything model to distinguish object boundaries. Lastly, we introduce a proposal-concept synchronized multiple-instance network, i.e., object mining and refinement with visual-semantic alignment, to discover objects matched to the text embeddings of concepts. Extensive experiments on Pascal VOC and MS COCO demonstrate that the proposed WSOVOD achieves new state-of-the-art compared with previous WSOD methods in both close-set object localization and detection tasks. Meanwhile, WSOVOD enables cross-dataset and open-vocabulary learning to achieve on-par or even better performance than well-established fully-supervised open-vocabulary object detection (FSOVOD).

3. Open-Vocabulary Object Detection Using Captions (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2020, cites: 579)
   Despite the remarkable accuracy of deep neural networks in object detection, they are costly to train and scale due to supervision requirements. Particularly, learning more object categories typically requires proportionally more bounding box annotations. Weakly supervised and zero-shot learning techniques have been explored to scale object detectors to more categories with less supervision, but they have not been as successful and widely adopted as supervised models. In this paper, we put forth a novel formulation of the object detection problem, namely open-vocabulary object detection, which is more general, more practical, and more effective than weakly supervised and zero-shot approaches. We propose a new method to train object detectors using bounding box annotations for a limited set of object categories, as well as image-caption pairs that cover a larger variety of objects at a significantly lower cost. We show that the proposed method can detect and localize objects for which no bounding box annotation is provided during training, at a significantly higher accuracy than zero-shot approaches. Meanwhile, objects with bounding box annotation can be detected almost as accurately as supervised methods, which is significantly better than weakly supervised baselines. Accordingly, we establish a new state of the art for scalable object detection.

4. Negative Deterministic Information-Based Multiple Instance Learning for Weakly Supervised Object Detection and Segmentation (IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems 2024, cites: 15)
   Weakly supervised object detection (WSOD) and semantic segmentation with image-level annotations have attracted extensive attention due to their high label efficiency. Multiple instance learning (MIL) offers a feasible solution for the two tasks by treating each image as a bag with a series of instances (object regions or pixels) and identifying foreground instances that contribute to bag classification. However, conventional MIL paradigms often suffer from issues, e.g., discriminative instance domination and missing instances. In this article, we observe that negative instances usually contain valuable deterministic information, which is the key to solving the two issues. Motivated by this, we propose a novel MIL paradigm based on negative deterministic information (NDI), termed NDI-MIL, which is based on two core designs with a progressive relation: NDI collection and negative contrastive learning (NCL). In NDI collection, we identify and distill NDI from negative instances online by a dynamic feature bank. The collected NDI is then utilized in a NCL mechanism to locate and punish those discriminative regions, by which the discriminative instance domination and missing instances issues are effectively addressed, leading to improved object- and pixel-level localization accuracy and completeness. In addition, we design an NDI-guided instance selection (NGIS) strategy to further enhance the systematic performance. Experimental results on several public benchmarks, including PASCAL VOC 2007, PASCAL VOC 2012, and MS COCO, show that our method achieves satisfactory performance. The code is available at: https://github.com/GC-WSL/NDI.

5. Localized Vision-Language Matching for Open-vocabulary Object Detection (German Conference on Pattern Recognition 2022, cites: 33)
   In this work, we propose an open-vocabulary object detection method that, based on image-caption pairs, learns to detect novel object classes along with a given set of known classes. It is a two-stage training approach that first uses a location-guided image-caption matching technique to learn class labels for both novel and known classes in a weakly-supervised manner and second specializes the model for the object detection task using known class annotations. We show that a simple language model fits better than a large contextualized language model for detecting novel objects. Moreover, we introduce a consistency-regularization technique to better exploit image-caption pair information. Our method compares favorably to existing open-vocabulary detection approaches while being data-efficient. Source code is available at https://github.com/lmb-freiburg/locov .

6. OvarNet: Towards Open-Vocabulary Object Attribute Recognition (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2023, cites: 62)
   In this paper, we consider the problem of simultaneously detecting objects and inferring their visual attributes in an image, even for those with no manual annotations provided at the training stage, resembling an open-vocabulary scenario. To achieve this goal, we make the following contributions: (i) we start with a naive two-stage approach for open-vocabulary object detection and attribute classification, termed CLIP-Attr. The candidate objects are first proposed with an offline RPN and later classified for semantic category and attributes; (ii) we combine all available datasets and train with a federated strategy to finetune the CLIP model, aligning the visual representation with attributes, additionally, we investigate the efficacy of leveraging freely available online image-caption pairs under weakly supervised learning; (iii) in pursuit of efficiency, we train a Faster-RCNN type model end-to-end with knowledge distillation, that performs class-agnostic object proposals and classification on semantic categories and attributes with classifiers generated from a text encoder; Finally, (iv) we conduct extensive experiments on VAW, MS-COCO, LSA, and OVAD datasets, and show that recognition of semantic category and attributes is complementary for visual scene understanding, i.e., jointly training object detection and attributes prediction largely outperform existing approaches that treat the two tasks independently, demonstrating strong generalization ability to novel attributes and categories.

7. Cross-Image Co-Attentive Instance Mining for Weakly Supervised Object Detection (2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Industrial Electronics for Sustainable Energy Systems (IESES) 2025, cites: 0)
   Weakly supervised object detection (WSOD), utilizing image-level labels, has attracted considerable attention for its ability to reduce annotation costs. Many approaches tackle the WSOD task as a multiple instance learning (MIL) problem. However, MIL algorithms are likely to concentrate on the most distinctive parts of an object rather than the whole object, which makes the optimization of the MIL algorithms prone to fall into local optima. This phenomenon can be caused by the lack of intra-class diversity within a single image. Concretely, utilizing only single intra-image information may cause networks to concentrate on the most common parts of objects within the same category, but overlooking their diverse parts. To address this challenge, we introduce the CrOss-image CoAttention (COCA) framework, designed to thoroughly investigate cross-image relationships for a more comprehensive extraction of object instances. Specifically, COCA includes a novel co-attention module that captures similarities and differences between objects across imagesa MIL-refinement module for generating pseudo labels, and a regression module for further improving location classification and regression based on pseudo labels. Without bells and whistles, COCA achieves a mean average precision (mAP) 6.4 points higher and a correct localization (CorLoc) 5.8 points higher than online instance classifier refinement (OICR) on PASCAL VOC 2007 datasets.

8. Multiple Instance Complementary Detection and Difficulty Evaluation for Weakly Supervised Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images (IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters 2023, cites: 18)
   Weakly supervised object detection (WSOD) in remote sensing images (RSIs) has attracted a lot of attention because it solely employs image-level labels to drive the model training. Most of the WSOD methods incline to mine salient object as positive instance, and the less salient objects are considered as negative instances, which will cause the problem of missing instances. In addition, the quantity of hard and easy instances is usually imbalanced, and consequently, the cumulative loss of a large amount of easy instances dominates the training loss, which limits the upper bound of WSOD performance. To handle the first problem, a complementary detection network (CDN) is proposed, which consists of a complementary multiple instance detection network (CMIDN) and a complementary feature learning (CFL) module. The CDN can capture robust complementary information from two basic multiple instance detection networks (MIDNs) and mine more object instances. To handle the second problem, an instance difficulty evaluation metric named instance difficulty score (IDS) is proposed, which is employed as the weight of each instance in the training loss. Consequently, the hard instances will be assigned larger weights according to the IDS, which can improve the upper bound of WSOD performance. The ablation experiments demonstrate that our method significantly increases the baseline method by large margins, i.e., 23.6% (10.2%) mean average precision (mAP) and 32.4% (13.1%) correct localization (CorLoc) gains on the NWPU VHR-10.v2 (DIOR) dataset. Our method obtains 58.1% (26.7%) mAP and 72.4% (47.9%) CorLoc on the NWPU VHR-10.v2 (DIOR) dataset, which achieves better performance compared with seven advanced WSOD methods.

9. C-MIL: Continuation Multiple Instance Learning for Weakly Supervised Object Detection (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2019, cites: 256)
   Weakly supervised object detection (WSOD) is a challenging task when provided with image category supervision but required to simultaneously learn object locations and object detectors. Many WSOD approaches adopt multiple instance learning (MIL) and have non-convex loss functions which are prone to get stuck into local minima (falsely localize object parts) while missing full object extent during training. In this paper, we introduce a continuation optimization method into MIL and thereby creating continuation multiple instance learning (C-MIL), with the intention of alleviating the non-convexity problem in a systematic way. We partition instances into spatially related and class related subsets, and approximate the original loss function with a series of smoothed loss functions defined within the subsets. Optimizing smoothed loss functions prevents the training procedure falling prematurely into local minima and facilitates the discovery of Stable Semantic Extremal Regions (SSERs) which indicate full object extent. On the PASCAL VOC 2007 and 2012 datasets, C-MIL improves the state-of-the-art of weakly supervised object detection and weakly supervised object localization with large margins.

   Existing instance segmentation models learn task-specific information using manual mask annotations from base (training) categories. These mask annotations require tremendous human effort, limiting the scalability to annotate novel (new) categories. To alleviate this problem, Open-Vocabulary (OV) methods leverage large-scale image-caption pairs and vision-language models to learn novel categories. In summary, an OV method learns task-specific information using strong supervision from base annotations and novel category information using weak supervision from image-captions pairs. This difference between strong and weak supervision leads to overfitting on base categories, resulting in poor generalization towards novel categories. In this work, we overcome this issue by learning both base and novel categories from pseudo-mask annotations generated by the vision-language model in a weakly supervised manner using our proposed Mask-free OVIS pipeline. Our method automatically generates pseudo-mask annotations by leveraging the localization ability of a pre-trained vision-language model for objects present in image-caption pairs. The generated pseudo-mask annotations are then used to supervise an instance segmentation model, freeing the entire pipeline from any labour-expensive instance-level annotations and overfitting. Our extensive experiments show that our method trained with just pseudo-masks significantly improves the mAP scores on the MS-COCO dataset and OpenImages dataset compared to the recent state-of-the-art methods trained with manual masks. Codes and models are provided in https://vibashan.github.io/ovis-web/.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.
